3: The narrator’s parenthetical identification of Bathsheba as ʾēšet ʾûriyyāh haḥittî — ‘the wife of Uriah the Hittite’ — is preserved verbatim in Matthew’s genealogy of Jesus (Matthew 1:6: ek tēs tou Ouriou, ‘by her of Uriah’). Matthew includes four women in the Messianic genealogy (Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, and ‘her of Uriah’), and in each case the inclusion emphasizes an irregular, scandalous, or Gentile connection through which the Messianic line passes. Bathsheba is designated not by her own name but by her murdered husband’s — which preserves the double crime (adultery and murder) in the genealogy without concealment. This is the genealogical argument against any claim that the Messianic ancestry validates human virtue: the lineage of the Son of God runs through precisely the moral catastrophes that would disqualify a merely human claim to righteousness. 1 Corinthians 1:27-28: ‘God chose what is low and despised in the world... so that no human being might boast in the presence of God.’ Romans 5:20: ‘where sin increased, grace abounded all the more.’ The retention of ‘wife of Uriah’ in the genealogy is the genealogical embodiment of this principle: grace is not embarrassed by the scandal; grace runs through it. The Messiah who is born of this lineage is the one who will bear not merely the genealogical weight of human sin but its full judicial weight — which is why the genealogy can afford to include what human constructions of honor would delete.
1: The narrative opens with temporal precision: 'In the spring of the year, the time when kings go out to battle.' David stays in Jerusalem while Joab leads the army — the narrator's first indictment. The Davidic king is called to lead his people, not delegate their danger while remaining at leisure. The contrast with Jesus is immediate: Christ does not remain at divine comfort while his people face spiritual warfare but enters the battle himself (Hebrews 2:14-15; John 12:27), and the greater David does not stay behind at the palace but leads the charge (Revelation 19:11). David's withdrawal from the field is the first step toward adultery and murder — confirming that spiritual idleness precedes moral catastrophe.
2: David rises from his bed in the late afternoon — already a temporal irregularity suggesting a disordered life — and walks on the palace roof where he sees a woman bathing. The roof-walk is privilege used for self-indulgence rather than watchman's duty (cf. Isaiah 62:6). The verb wayyarʾ (he saw) is morally laden in the OT — the same verb as in Genesis 3:6 ('when the woman saw that the tree was good') and Genesis 6:2 ('the sons of God saw the daughters of men'). Unchecked seeing leads to wanting; wanting to taking. This verse enacts Jesus's warning in Matthew 5:28: 'everyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery with her in his heart.' David at his worst is the anti-type against which Christ's perfect obedience stands.
4: David sends messengers, takes Bathsheba, lies with her, and she conceives — four swift verbs enacting the descent from covetousness to adultery. The grammar resists lingering: the act is narrated with brutal economy, and the narrator adds 'for she had purified herself from her uncleanness' to make clear that she was ritually clean and therefore the conception is David's, not her husband's. David uses his royal power to violate a covenant-law that he himself was meant to embody (Deuteronomy 17:17, 'he shall not acquire many wives'). The king as predator inverts the covenant order; the true King, by contrast, lays down his life for his Bride rather than taking from her (Ephesians 5:25). David's sin here generates the entire crisis of Nathan's parable (2 Samuel 12), where David = rich man, Uriah = poor man, Bathsheba = the single ewe lamb.
5: Bathsheba sends word: 'I am pregnant.' The brief message is the pivot of the narrative — David's private sin now has a public consequence he must manage. This is the moment when concealment replaces repentance, and a chain of deceptions and violence begins. The contrast with the Annunciation (Luke 1:31) is instructive: both are messages about a miraculous conception, but Bathsheba's pregnancy signals guilt and sets in motion a cover-up, while Mary's pregnancy signals grace and unfolds into redemption. The pregnancy that must be hidden is the antitype; the pregnancy that is proclaimed to the world is the fulfillment. David's response to the news is manipulation; God's response to human sin is Incarnation.
6: David sends to Joab to bring Uriah home from the battlefield — the king's first attempt at cover-up, using military logistics to arrange a conjugal visit that would make the pregnancy plausibly Uriah's. The irony deepens as the narrative proceeds: Uriah will prove more righteous than David in every exchange. David, the covenant-keeper who danced before the ark (2 Samuel 6), now attempts to manipulate one of his most loyal servants into unwitting complicity in deception. Uriah's integrity will inadvertently expose David's corruption. The type-pattern anticipates the gospel: the righteous man (Uriah) is ultimately destroyed to cover the powerful man's sin — a preview of the legal mechanism of Christ's passion where the innocent dies for the guilty, though in the gospel the innocent dies voluntarily.
7: Uriah arrives and David asks about the welfare of Joab, the army, and the war — a facade of royal pastoral concern that masks the actual agenda. The irony intensifies because Uriah's report about his comrades is the very service David has abdicated (v1). Uriah is fighting; David should be fighting. Uriah cares about the welfare of the troops; David is using his troops as pawns. This inversion of covenantal roles recapitulates the Adam-Christ contrast: Adam, charged to guard the garden, abdicated to the serpent's agenda; Christ, the new Adam, guards his flock at the cost of his own life (John 10:11). David in 2 Samuel 11 is Adam in the garden — the appointed guardian who becomes the transgressor.
8: David tells Uriah to go home and wash his feet — a euphemism (and perhaps a literal invitation to enjoy marital relations after the fatigue of the march). David sends a present after him, a generous royal gift that is actually bait in a trap. The gift of food and hospitality designed to deceive echoes the corrupted hospitality motif running through the OT (Genesis 26:10; Judg 19) — covenant hospitality turned predatory. Uriah's response in v9 makes David's gift irrelevant: Uriah refuses to go home. The loyal soldier's refusal of the king's 'gift' actually exposes the gift as manipulation, a pattern repeated in the wilderness temptation where Satan offers Jesus what is already his by right (Matthew 4:8-9).
9: Uriah sleeps at the door of the king's house with all the servants of his lord, refusing to go home to his wife. The verb wayyiškāḇ — he slept — is the same root as David's sin (wayyiškāḇ ʿimmāh, 'he lay with her,' v4), but here it is entirely innocent: Uriah lies down at the gate in solidarity with the soldiers who have no home to go to. The contrast is precise: David's bed is the site of sin; Uriah's sleeping place is the site of covenant loyalty. The Hittite soldier embodies the covenant ethos of ḥeseḏ that David has abandoned. This moral inversion — the Gentile more faithful than the Israelite king — runs from Rahab and Ruth to the Centurion of Matthew 8:10 and Cornelius of Acts 10.
10: David learns that Uriah did not go home — his cover-up plan has failed. He summons Uriah to interrogate him. The king cross-examines his own victim, a grotesque parody of the judicial role that the king was meant to embody (Proverbs 16:10). David is simultaneously judge and criminal, the very corruption that Ps 82 indicts ('How long will you judge unjustly?'). The true David, by contrast, is the judge who himself undergoes judgment for his people (Isaiah 53:8; 2 Corinthians 5:21) — he does not exploit the asymmetry of power but inverts it, accepting condemnation on behalf of those he should condemn. David's interrogation of Uriah is one of Scripture's most searing portraits of power's corruption.
11: Uriah's speech is the moral center of 2 Samuel 11: 'The ark and Israel and Judah dwell in tents, and my lord Joab and the servants of my lord are camping in the open field. Shall I then go to my house to eat and to drink and to lie with my wife? As you live and as your soul lives, I will not do this thing.' Uriah's oath is by David's life — the king he is about to shame by contrast. The theological key is the ark: Uriah will not enjoy domestic comfort while the ark (YHWH's covenant presence) is in a tent. The soldier's theology of solidarity with YHWH's cause exceeds his king's. The antitype is Christ's kenosis (Philippians 2:6-7) — the Son does not cling to the comfort of heaven while his people languish; he empties himself and camps in the flesh.
12: David asks Uriah to stay another day and he will send him back — buying time for a second strategy (the drinking party of v13) after the first cover-up failed. Each additional day of delay accumulates moral debt: David continues to use Uriah without telling him the truth, treating this loyal soldier as an instrument rather than a person. The instrumental use of persons violates the imago Dei principle that every human being is made in God's image and is not reducible to a tool (Genesis 1:27; James 3:9). The contrast with Christ's treatment of his followers is absolute: Jesus lays down his life for his servants (John 15:13), turning the master-servant hierarchy upside down (Mark 10:45).
13: David gets Uriah drunk — a final attempt to overcome his conscience through wine. Even intoxicated, Uriah does not go home but sleeps again among the servants. Uriah's integrity survives impairment: even his unconscious impulses are faithful. The typological force of Uriah's consistent refusal across all three strategies (invitation, gift, wine) is that no external pressure can corrupt a man of genuine covenant loyalty. This is the exact obedience that Adam failed in the garden (temptation overcame him) and that Christ demonstrated in Gethsemane — not my will but yours (Luke 22:42). Uriah is a foil who reveals by contrast what covenant faithfulness actually looks like under pressure.
14: David writes a letter to Joab and sends it by Uriah's own hand — the most cynical detail in the narrative. Uriah carries his own death sentence without knowing it, trusting the king whose trust he has earned. This is the moment that transforms adultery into a premeditated murder conspiracy. The sealed letter is a close parallel to the written death-order against Uriah that Bathsheba later reads with horror. The image of the innocent man delivering his own death warrant appears in Apocryphal traditions and typologically adumbrates the Passion: the innocent Christ is handed over by his own people ('he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him,' John 1:11), and his own disciples carry the betrayal money (Matthew 26:14-15).
15: The letter's content is explicit: 'Set Uriah in the forefront of the hardest fighting, and then draw back from him, that he may be struck down and die.' This is premeditated judicial murder using military means — an assassination disguised as battlefield mishap. David's command to Joab makes Joab a partner in the crime, implicating the entire command structure of the army. The command reveals the full depth of David's corruption: the king who was meant to execute justice is executing injustice against the most loyal of his servants. The antitype is not merely the contrast with Christ's innocence but the specific mechanism of the Passion: the authorities who put Jesus to death also used official structures to accomplish what was actually murder (John 19:12-16; Acts 2:23).
16: Joab follows the order, placing Uriah at the position where he knows the defenders are strongest. Joab's compliance reveals that the king's moral failure has corrupted his commander. One man's sin descends through the chain of command, implicating subordinates in guilt they might not have chosen freely. The systemic spread of David's sin anticipates Paul's analysis of Adam's sin: 'by the one man's disobedience the many were made sinners' (Romans 5:19). The commander who once fought YHWH's wars faithfully now engineers the death of an innocent man at the king's behest. Sin in leadership is not contained — it flows downward and outward through all who serve the compromised leader.
17: The men of the city (Ammonite defenders) come out and fight Joab's forces — Uriah the Hittite is among those who fall in the battle. The death itself is embedded in the larger casualty report that Joab will send, making it appear incidental. This is the mechanism David designed: collective military failure masking targeted assassination. The theological weight is that YHWH allows this moment of successful wickedness — David achieves his immediate goal — because the full judgment will come through Nathan (2 Samuel 12). The apparent success of evil is a consistent OT pattern (Lamentations 3:37-38) that prepares for the cross: the crucifixion appears to be the triumph of wickedness, but it is the most paradoxical act of redemptive justice in all of history (Acts 2:23; 1 Corinthians 2:8).
18: Joab sends a messenger to David with a full report of the battle — embedding the news of Uriah's death within a larger account of military operations, as David requested. The report strategy reveals the conspiracy's sophistication: David wanted deniability, a death that looked like war. But the narrator and the reader know the truth, and more importantly, YHWH knows (v27: 'the thing that David had done displeased YHWH'). The hidden knowledge of God that watches human concealment is a theme throughout Scripture (Genesis 16:13; Psalms 44:21; Hebrews 4:13), and it grounds the doctrine of divine judgment: nothing is hidden that will not be revealed (Luke 12:2-3), and the Judge of all the earth will do right (Genesis 18:25).
19: Joab prepares the messenger with a hypothetical royal rebuke about approaching the city wall too closely, drawn from the story of Abimelech at Thebez (Judges 9:50-54). Joab uses a historical example of military folly as rhetorical inoculation — anticipating David's anger and defusing it before the name 'Uriah' is mentioned. The intertextual reference to Abimelech is ironic: Abimelech was killed by a woman's millstone in an illegitimate siege — a death that Scripture records as divine judgment. Joab reaches for a cautionary tale of military hubris and its consequences, not realizing that the Uriah situation is itself subject to divine judgment far exceeding the rebuke David might give for tactical recklessness.
20: Joab instructs the messenger: if the king becomes angry about the approach to the city wall, remind him of Abimelech. The conversation that Joab scripts for the messenger is a rehearsal of how to deflect royal anger through historical analogy — sophisticated court politics in the service of murder concealment. The court culture capable of such maneuvering is the product of accumulated compromise: no one around David corrects him at this moment; everyone is complicit in managing consequences rather than confronting sin. The absence of a prophetic voice in 2 Samuel 11 (Nathan does not appear until 2 Samuel 12) parallels the silence before the storm — judgment is gathering while the court busies itself with administrative crisis management.
21: The reference to Abimelech's death by a woman's millstone at Thebez (Judges 9:50-54) serves Joab's rhetorical strategy, but its echo in this context is charged: Abimelech died as a consequence of his violent and illegitimate reign, struck down by a woman he had terrorized. David is about to receive a similar judgment by a different means — through Nathan's prophetic parable in 2 Samuel 12, and through the sword that 'will never depart from your house' (2 Samuel 12:10). The OT habit of recycling historical cautionary tales into new contexts reveals how biblical narrative accumulates moral weight: each new act of violence or injustice is judged against the accumulated record of how God has dealt with similar acts before.
22: The messenger travels and comes to David and gives him the full report — the mechanics of communication across the narrative underline the normalcy within which evil is being transacted. No supernatural darkness descends; the sun continues to shine; messengers travel normally. The banality of the evil in 2 Samuel 11 — war continues, messages are sent, plans are implemented — prefigures Hannah Arendt's observation that great evil operates through ordinary bureaucratic processes. The prophet will call it out in 2 Samuel 12, but until then the machinery of court life masks the magnitude of what has been done. The plain recitation of events is itself the narrator's indictment: everything is proceeding normally, and everything is catastrophically wrong.
23: The messenger reports the battle's course — the Ammonites prevailed in the open field, the Israelites were driven back toward the gate, but the archers shot from the wall. The tactical detail establishes that Joab's army paid a real price beyond Uriah to accomplish the king's cover-up: other Israelite soldiers died in the artificially exposed position. David's sin has cost innocent lives beyond Uriah's. The cost of sin in Scripture is never confined to the guilty party — it spreads to the innocent who are caught in its wake. This dynamic reaches its theological apex in Christ who, though himself innocent, absorbs the cost of human sin that has spread to all of creation (Romans 8:19-22).
24: The messenger concludes by naming Uriah the Hittite among those who died — a subordinate clause embedded in the casualty report, as Joab had designed. The matter-of-fact delivery of Uriah's death, sandwiched between military statistics, is the final touch of David's conspiracy. The king has achieved what he wanted: Uriah is dead, and it looks like war. But the narrative has positioned the reader to know the truth; and YHWH, who sees through every human device (Proverbs 15:3; Psalms 139:1-4), sees through this one. The concealed murder will be exposed by a shepherd parable from a prophet — the Word of God penetrating the court's conspiracy with a story about a lamb (2 Samuel 12:1-4).
25: David's message back to Joab — 'Do not let this trouble you; the sword devours now this one and now that one' — is a cynical reduction of Uriah's targeted murder to the randomness of battlefield death. The platitude 'the sword devours one or another' is not false as a general statement, but as David's response to the news of the man he ordered killed, it is a lie. David consoles Joab for doing what David commanded him to do, confirming that the cover-up is now a shared secret between them. The moral distance David is placing between himself and the act ('the sword devours') is precisely the kind of language that prophetic confrontation must pierce — Nathan in 2 Samuel 12 will remove every layer of abstraction and say simply: 'You are the man' (2 Samuel 12:7).
26: When Bathsheba hears that Uriah her husband is dead, she mourns for him — the Hebrew text specifies that she 'lamented' (wattispoḏ) her husband, the formal mourning rite. The brief notice of her grief is the narrator's humanizing of Bathsheba: she is not a passive object but a wife who has lost her husband to the machinations of a king who should have protected him. The lamentation also marks time — the period of mourning must pass before the next step in David's cover-up can proceed. The rhythm of death and mourning and then David's swift action to take her reveals David's impatience: he cannot even allow the full mourning period before making Bathsheba part of the cover-up by marriage.
27: When the mourning was over, David sent and brought Bathsheba to his house; she became his wife and bore him a son. The narrative then adds the theological verdict: 'But the thing that David had done displeased YHWH.' The final sentence is the entire theological weight of the chapter — everything that seemed to go according to plan, every cover-up that apparently succeeded, is undone by four Hebrew words: wayyēraʿ bĕʿênê YHWH — 'it was evil in the eyes of YHWH.' The divine commentary lands after the human story has apparently resolved itself: David has what he wanted, Uriah is dead, Bathsheba is queen, the child is on the way. But YHWH's evaluation reframes everything. The typological contrast with Christ is total: David achieves his will against God's; Christ achieves God's will against his own comfort (Luke 22:42).
